chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: Hello, mate. How's it going, Zach? I'm definitely here this time. No worries.

ZACH: I appreciate it, man. Hey, thank you for taking the time and hopping on and rescheduling as well. No, no, no.

TABARAK: All good, man. Sorry, I just completely left you hanging last time. Oh, man.

ZACH: Sounds like you've got a busy schedule. Yeah, yeah. When the little one goes down, all hands on deck.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: TABARAK: Yeah, I hear you. So I guess maybe let me just kind of give an outline of what I wanted to go over today. Maybe some quick intros and then essentially give you an overview. I am a new CEO building a product out right now for Fractions. And so essentially my co-founder and I, we, maybe I'll give you a little bit of background. My name is Tabarik. I used to do sales in the corporate world for about 20 years. I mean, from Apple to Verizon, and then recently as a regional vice president for new sales at DocuSign for about six or seven years. And so I left that corporate world and naturally in between that world and starting my business now, we started doing some consulting. And as we started consulting, we found that I was doing a lot more clients for essentially like new startups, helping them with go to market, some sales stuff, some marketing stuff. And we realized it was more than ever just giving them advice. We're just getting in there and actually doing the work. And I realized I just cannot manage multiple clients and then essentially grow my business, the AI business that I'm trying to work on. Right. And so we found this insight. We didn't know if it was true. So we started reaching out to a whole bunch of fractionals and kind of getting an idea of like, is this our bias or are people handling the situation in a certain way?

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: TABARAK: Right. So that kind of led me to perusing LinkedIn. I found yours. And based on some of our hypothesis, it looked like you're really busy. You have a ton of clients that you work with. You have a foot in building a product plus a community. And so I thought, wow. Well, you know, and then I stumbled. Yeah. This guy's got the problem, but he could also sell it to another thousand people as well. Let's get this guy on board. I mean, that's a huge thing, too. I mean, you're a sales guy, right? But the reality was what sealed the deal for me is when I got to your exact your Delphi agent and I started talking to that and I was like, well, I'm getting really good feedback. Let me talk to this guy in person. Right. Because there's a level of your earned knowledge that you've had and also what you put into the exact box. So I guess the whole purpose of this, I wanted to kind of learn a little bit more about you. Go over some of the problems I'm seeing in the market and then show you some ways I'm hearing other fractions are dealing with it. Get your kind of advice and take on that and then give you an overview, just a quick overview of what we're trying to build and then see if you have any interest on any next steps. Like, would you want to? Because ultimately, what we really want to do is build a customer development program, get key fractionals in there that are in alignment that would help want to shape the product, get the product in their hands and use it. Right. So that's kind of my my overview. But what about you, Zach? Why don't you give me a little bit of background about yourself?

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: ZACH: Yeah, sure. And look, I think all of your all of your kind of instincts or insights there are bang on. Mate, I'll give you the I'll try to give it as short as possible, but you know how it is with our sales guy, which we like to talk. I've been in tech for a long time. Tech sales, mostly sales and management. My last real job with a regular salary was for a late stage ad tech company called MediaMath out of Singapore. We came back to Australia just in time for Covid. We launched AI healthcare startup. Let me put your notes in. Got to get those notes. Yeah. Yeah. Launched a startup, raised a bit of money, got a couple of clients, never quite managed to hit escape velocity. And so fast forward into that kind of two years in, I then had two kids under two, was looking to work flexibly remotely from a little seaside town.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: Where are you, man? You're in Seattle.

ZACH: No, I moved to Bangkok. I live in Bangkok now.

TABARAK: Oh, you're in Bangkok. I left Seattle, went on this world tour, ended up in Bangkok and realized, well, I can get my life together. Let me let me do what I really want to do. You know, I got an office and set up shop here. I'm just going to chat.

ZACH: Yeah. Mate, that's super interesting. I do know some interesting fractionals for you to talk to in Bangkok, too. Mostly U.S. expats doing CMO work, living the dream, selling back in with American clients, living like absolute kings, living that arbitrage, that exchange arbitrage dream in Bangkok. So remind me that that's a point to introduce you to those guys as well.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: ZACH: So I just started kind of doing fractional work by accident. I'd work with fractional CFOs and even a fractional CTO. I didn't know anyone who was doing fractional sales work. So I just started shouting about it, picked up a couple of clients, which is obviously good validation. This is about four years ago now. Obviously, it's had a bit of a moment since then and it's kind of popped off. And along the way, a whole bunch of people reached out. In fact, way more people who wanted to be a fractional rather than hire one. And similar kind of questions, man. You've poked around on the site, you've done research, you know what it is. It was all the operations side of things. How does this fractional thing work? How do you find clients? How do you split your time? How do you know what to work on? What contract do you use? Things like that.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
ZACH: And so that was the birth of the fractional exec community. Started as a free slack group, grew up a little bit about 18 months ago, evolved to a paid membership model on a dedicated community platform called Mining Networks. Still very much focused on the operational side. How do people do that? So that's still there. It's still a side hustle. It's 2% of my revenue, if that.

TABARAK: How much of your time?

ZACH: Yeah, way too much. That revenue to time split is fucking wrong, dude. If you look at the stuff that I talk about and all the content, it's way out of whack.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: ZACH: My day job is still fractional go-to-market work. I'll embed a couple of days a week, one or two days a week with a client to scale out their sales and go-to-market engine. So, yeah, mate, that's kind of where I'm at. In terms of the number one problem that fractionals have, the one they're all dealing with, it's generating pipeline consistently. The feast or famine cycle, the single shingle operator was the catchy phrase that somebody dropped on me, and that rings true. I've got my clients. I can do plenty of pipe. Then I'm full. I've got two or three clients. I'm booked out. And then, yeah, three to six months later and come out, it's like, shit, where's my pipeline? I haven't done anything. I haven't posted. I haven't done any outreach.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: ZACH: So that's kind of where it's at. So, mate, very happy to kind of dig in and hear what you're thinking and throw that at you. I will say that selling to fractionals is fraught because none of them have any money. And I can give you some inside tip. The best time to sell to fractionals is either just before they leave their corporate life and go into fractional or just after, because they're still cashed up and they think that dropping 500 bucks or a thousand bucks or something on a tool or a community or whatever is nothing. And I spend more than that on the corporate expense account. I don't know how they're going to be in 12 to 18 months of not having a regular income. So that's the time to strike. If you can figure out when that is, get in then. And that's the place to be.
chunks.adoption_barrier: fractionals have inconsistent income; price sensitivity grows 12-18 months in

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: TABARAK: That's really good insight. And I, OK, well, I mean, I guess maybe I can just kind of hop in this. Here's a question for you, because, you know, you've been in it, you've been a fractional, you've talked to all these people. What is your problem? Because, I mean, you come from sales. And and when I think about root cause of not getting clients, it's the same thing as sales, right? You were very interested because you were prospecting three months ago. Right. And you start prospecting. Right. So that's kind of a piece that I understand that, you know. But what about for you specifically? Are you having those same issues or other issues that you're dealing with?

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: ZACH: Yeah, mate, that's like if I had if I had the magic wand, if you could solve one thing, it's that pipeline consistently. And mate, I, you know, I know all the tools and I'm deep

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: ZACH: But there's the losing the client trust piece is one that I've I've learned the hard way. And it's really a process where every week we do a weekly stand up without fail. And it's, you know, even if it's just 30 minutes, it's here's what we worked on this week. Here's what we're working on. Next week, just so that there's no slippage. And if the client's too busy, then you get kind of drift and then they get very unhappy.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: The other one, which kind of sits under a couple of these as well, is developing a playbook. So, you know, having doing this for four years now, it's only now kind of getting to the point where I have a playbook of I know what I'm going to do with the client. There are kind of six areas that we'll have a look at. We'll identify almost like a gap analysis. Here's the six areas and I'll roll that out. And so having that playbook means I can solve a lot of this just having that itself.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: But then I can also embed that into I use Claude code to run my entire life. So, you know, I've now got a flow where it's a new client. It spins up the folder structure, does some deep research. I start building that context stack. I've got a bunch of frameworks. I've got a bunch of examples, a bunch of templates. And so that allows me to I got that system for when the shit hits the fan midweek. I'm not feeling overwhelmed or anxious because I don't know what I'm doing and I don't know how to let the client down because I got that in place. Urgent work blows up and it's OK. And I can refer to, you know, here's what we agreed on high level. But I've got flexibility in there so we can shift it. And, you know, commitments still slip. That's that's part of the game. You know, I tend to work with early stage companies. So what's on fire this week might be different from next week, let alone next month.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: TABARAK: How many clients do you run right now? What's your ideal amount? If you could, if you could run them perfectly.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: ZACH: Yeah. So, mate, ideally, maximum kind of client facing time is kind of three, four days a week because there's just a whole bunch of admin that comes on top of that as well. And, you know, if I wanted to do 100 hour weeks, I'd go back to tech life and get paid a lot more and sleep a lot better. So, yeah, sometimes that fluctuates. I have and it will depend on the shape of the clients as well. So it's generally one or two days a week. You know, I've done a client that was three days a week for a sprint, but more than three. And context switching is is a real challenge. And that's where. At the moment, I've got a couple of coaching clients as well. I've got three or four coaching clients with three fractional clients. I wouldn't have been able to do that without code to run that kind of context for me.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: So technically you're at like six people right now.

ZACH: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.

TABARAK: And that's what I've been finding, just so you know, it's that I find different tiers. There's people that are stuck about two to three and then their systems just don't really get past that. And there's people that have been in the game eight, nine years to figure out a whole bunch of systems, like you said. And they've gotten up to six to seven. And I've realized that some of those people are pushing for more. But they have a combination of retainer versus project based kind of like.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: ZACH: Yeah. Yeah. Again, that's another very hard one lesson and some good conversations in the community around, you know, start with a half day workshop. Then do a couple of weeks consulting, move up to the retainer model. And in hindsight, I've dropped some really good opportunities by being too rigid about, you know, I won't get out of bed unless you commit to a three month retainer or something like that. It's great if you can do it. But if your pipeline is full, then you shouldn't be doing it.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: TABARAK: Exactly. Well, here's some of the solutions that we've been hearing. Every morning coming in and spending like 30 to 60 minutes to triage and prioritize your day because things have changed. And you just want to make sure like what's on fire and what's not for that overwhelmed piece. It's just working more, working at nights, working weekends, working reactively, not strategically. Say no to new clients, kind of that two to three range. Right. When it comes to retainers and depending on the mix of retainer versus, you know, just advisory roles that can change. One thing with the urgent week blows up in your weeks, we're just realizing people just aren't doing their goals. And that's pipeline stuff, too. Right. Like you have a goal. You need to do X amount of pipeline. You put it off. I'm doing this next time. I'm doing this later. Right. So rather than, you know, block off that time or find that strategy or commit to yourself, we're just seeing that more often. And then we're seeing people with a ton of different tools. And part of it is some of your work lives and your clients tools that they require. And some of it's your stuff that you require. Some things you have for other systems. You're all over the place. You don't really have one command center. Right. And then you're just spending five, at least five hours a week, just moving stuff around and taking care of stuff, you know. And we saw that as some of that. Do any of these solutions fit? Do you see any of these being, you know, you've learned how to get around them or are they just kind of mixed in with some of the stuff you've been working on and dealing with?

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: ZACH: Yeah, probably. I think a lot of this is built into kind of how I work. And because I'm. Because I'm pretty deep down the Claude Code rabbit hole, a lot of this is kind of woven into what I do. So, you know, the daily triaging I'll have, I got a morning sweep agent that has a look at everything I need to do, both from line and from client activity. Make definitely, you know, when the shit hits the fan working nights, you know, I kept clients quarterly goals. Client goals generally get better down if it gets busy. My kind of working on the business tends to get pushed as well in terms of tool stack. Yeah, man, I've got notion. I've kind of played with most of all of those. But again, Claude Code is becoming my co-pilot for everything. And it plugs into kind of all the tools and increasingly I'm working less in the tools and just talking to Claude Code and getting it to do it.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: TABARAK: Yeah, it looks like you're kind of finding your groove in the technology space. When I was thinking, so I guess what tools are you seeing a lot of other standard tools that you're seeing fractional use? I mean, I'm hearing a lot of notion. But are there other tools that you hear that most people actually just aggregate to?

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: ZACH: Again, notion is a big one, but it's pretty varied, to be honest. It's everything from, you know, Asana, notion, click up, Monday, usually whatever they kind of bring across. A lot of people just working in sheets, working in spreadsheets as well. It's not like it's not big numbers. You know, if you look at if you consider pipeline, for example, my average average client is three to six months. And so I only need two or three. So, you know, at most, I probably need 10 to 12 good clients a year. And so it's not a high volume game like I use for CRM. I use Adio, but I'm bad at like, you know, like most salespeople, I'm bad at keeping it up to date. And it's pretty minimal. I just use, you know, I just use out of the box functionality because it's not a big volume game. You just keep it in your head. You know what I mean?
chunks.adoption_barrier: low volume pipeline doesn't justify keeping CRM updated; just keep it in your head

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: TABARAK: How many reach outs have you figured out your conversion from prospecting to client typically?

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: ZACH: I'm not doing any sustained outbound at the moment, which, again, is fucking nuts. To give you an idea, I had called in the last couple of weeks. I had 40 discovery calls that was pretty evenly split, 20 for the community, 24 fractional sales work. In terms of how many fractional clients will land, I would say probably around, too early to tell, but probably no more than two or three. So there is a fairly, you know, there's a lot of education and there's a lot of people reaching out who aren't ICP and I don't do any qualification pre-call. And that's a pretty high number, higher than it normally is, because a lot of the content that I've been pumping out around using AI for go to market and keeping it fully practical is a hot topic right now. And that seems to be kind of resonating.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: TABARAK: Have you, I mean, you haven't done any outbounds, but have you played with ads at all, LinkedI

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: For sure. For sure. Now, I totally understand that. We got into the marketing game. We got into the ads game. And I'm realizing it just depends on your ICP and it depends on the capacity. Like you said, it's such a low volume game. You know, sometimes you don't need that large scope if you're not even able to do the smaller part. All right. So here's our approach. We're building a tool. And kind of think about it. It's like Google Maps for your work week. It finds the fastest route and reroutes around fives. So you always know exactly what to do next. And essentially what you've been talking about, Laquan, it's essentially that same idea. That thing that you're working on now, one of our main core things that we work on at the highest level is we look at the first principles. And I think a large thing is capacity. Everything relies on do you know how big your tasks are, how long they are, how they're estimated, how much time you actually have. Those are the core principles. What is your work on the backdrop of your time? And then there's other factors on, like, what is important versus urgent, right? And having an algorithm that essentially takes you into consideration and actually puts you at the forefront. Like you said, when things get crazy, my client work happens first before me. That's one of our main key premises is that you've got to put your mask on first. And we build around that, that your goals need to be prior. I mean, obviously your goals need to matter, but we also need to take into consideration all the fires that happen, right? So essentially what we do, we pull all your work from every system into one spot. We understand your goals, your client goals, your priorities. We break large commitments into clear next steps. We plan your week based on real capacity. We replan instantly when a fire breaks out across any of the clients. We protect your highest value commitments from slipping. And we show you the most important thing to do next, even when your week is on fire, right, even from moment to moment. And then we also help you track your weekly goals and your client goals so that we know what is important. What do you think of this approach? And would you pay for something like this if you didn't build it yourself?

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: Yeah, I guess it depends on like where are you pulling that data from? Like how do you know what my tasks are? How do you know how much time it's going to take? Because like always, man, the reason that Claude has been successful for me is it removes the friction of putting shit in there. Do you know what I mean? Because I just hit double tap and I just talk at it. Yeah, I got this task. I'm going to do this and add that in. And like, yeah, build this document and then pull that out. Yeah, go and get the granola transcript and let me know what to do I have on that. And so it removes a lot of that friction. So how you could go about kind of getting people to import that data and where you're kind of where you're doing this, where you're pulling that from would be interesting.
chunks.adoption_barrier: friction of inputting tasks into a system; voice/no-friction capture is what makes Claude work

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: I've used it. It's pretty common in the community as well. There's those calendar tools like Reclaim. Is it Reclaim AI? If you looked at that, you're nodding. It's not at this level, but it's more like time blocking. Yeah. They're actually pretty relatively common in the fractional space. I've tried that because I'm very bad at time management, but I end up with calendar overwhelm. And so I end up backwards. I just ignore it because then I'm not doing that. I don't feel like doing that. And then I switch it off. So I think if you could get that part right, if you could remove that friction for import, I think it would be pretty useful. If you had a way to kind of surface, here's what's important. Okay, today, these are the things you need to work on. You've got a block in your calendar. You've got two hours here. Do that for some deep work for a client. You're away at a conference next week. So just be aware. You've got to get this shit done before then.
chunks.adoption_barrier: calendar tools create overwhelm; he ignores them and switches them off when overloaded

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: Absolutely. I think one of our big premises and what we're realizing is that people don't want an AI overlord. They don't want AI to do everything. You want to be in the cockpit to be able to say, hey, serve me my best decisions, and I'll choose the one that makes the best sense, right? And so I think that's our approach on that. It's offering that piece. And to your point, too, it's allowing you to choose what you want in the system, ideally for us to understand your capacity. We didn't know all the work you have and what's available for you in time, right? So you can block off your calendars to say, hey, I sleep during this time. I have date nights on Saturday. Don't touch any of that. So then we know what time we're playing with, and you can only play with that time, right? And, yeah, I mean, that's kind of our approach. I don't know if it's in full alignment. I can say that you're pretty advanced for the type of customer that might fit in this, but I think you can give us really good feedback because you've played the alternative. I mean, reality is I won't even sugarcoat it. It's a lot of the stuff you're doing with Cloud. The difference is we're fine-tuning it straight to the context of a fraction, right? And when it comes to the time estimates, Cloud's not going to know all of that. So we train all the tasks. We take the tasks. We understand it. We break it down. We estimate it based on the user. And then when you do the task again, we re-estimate it so we know how the time actually is for you consistently, and we help you understand and engage that time so the system gets smarter every time, right? And that's the difference with Cloud. It just won't be able to – they have such a large business. They're not going to do all that for you, right? So we're essentially taking all that and centering it around you in other fractions, learning from other people, and just a sneak peek for you eventually down the road. One part of it, and it's our first level, is can we manage – can we give you the right thing to do? And then obviously when we figure that out, there's a lot to go into that, capacity, your tasks, all of that. Then after that is can we give you the right thing to do and help you do it the right way, right? So those playbooks that you're talking about would be our next steps in there, right? How do we allow you to input your playbooks? And then so our capacity engine, our agent, will be able to know, Zach, here's – this is for you. And, well, your agent here can take care of this work for you. It knows this part. And so now you can distribute your capacity amongst you and your agent. But it's a step-by-step process that we're trying to build out, and we have to crack the time in giving the right thing first before we can do all the other stuff in our opinion.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: Interesting.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: So I guess that's all I have. I know we just ran across our time. I leave it to you, Zach. If you're interested and you'd like any follow-up, if you'd like to see the product, I mean I'm happy to give you a demonstration so that you can see what it is. I would love your feedback. I don't know if you are the exact right fit for the stage and where we're at, but if you would want any information or learn from that or give us any feedback, I'm happy for that.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: I'm happy to continue to give feedback. And, look, I can also categorically state I am an outlier in terms of my insane use of Claude Cohen. I've been completely Claude-pilled, and I've been wildly unsuccessful in getting other fractionals to kind of get to this level. So I think there's a piece there. Have you connected with other fractional groups as well?
chunks.adoption_barrier: other fractionals don't adopt AI tools at his depth

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: I've signed up for a few groups, but I haven't gotten into talking to a ton of them. Right now we've been doing a lot of LinkedIn DMs. When I started strategizing for our ICP choices, this was maybe about six months ago. And so we started by adding 100 fractionals a week, me and my co-founder, every week, right, for months now. And so now our pipeline is pretty big, and then we've been prospecting into them for the past few weeks. And we've gotten really good response rates. We're just trying to get it to the core thing is trying to find the core group of evangelists, right? They use a lot of product tools. They see the problem. They're excited to solve a problem. They would like to kind of shape a product, because I think that's the best people to build a product around, so we can get some really good feedback from that area and also pay it forward to them and solve their problem, because you're not a developer, right? You're not going to create an entire product for everybody. For us, it's like let us do the work of getting it developed, and then let us get you the product that fits you best, right?

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: Yeah, totally. Makes sense, right? There is a group in Asia called Portfolio Careers Asia, headed by Moritz. I'll send it to you on LinkedIn.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: Oh, and then you said at the beginning of the call, you said there's some fractionals, you know, in Bangkok. That would be really cool.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: No, no, all good, man, all good. Yeah, mate, when I wake up at 3 a.m. and the name of that UK company comes to mind, I'll see if I can dig them up as well. I don't know if they're – in fact, they're definitely not – maybe they are when you get a bit more further down the track, but they're a marketplace. They seem to be doing pretty well. Many have tried and failed in this space. I've seen quite a few drop large amounts of money on it, and it hasn't worked out.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: There was one, actually – there was one who built a kind of a tool that was end-to-end workflow. So it was we'll help you do the pitching, we'll do the contract, we'll do the signing, then you manage the project. And the thing that they did that was really clever was it was free to use, and if you brought your own work, you could still use the tool and just run your own clients. But if you got a client from their marketplace, they would take their clip of 15 or 20 percent or whatever it was. It still wasn't good enough for them to be successful, but maybe they were just too early. Maybe they should have charged a little bit more.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. I think it's just hard when you're clipping the ticket like that. You know the thing is – and you know this, you're a sales guy. It's like how much value can you honestly deliver them, right? And then how do you put that value in their face so they know it is? Even in sales, I've given people 10x value and they still refuse to pay, right? So it's understanding the persona, right? I think everything in this space with AI, you've got to have the right distribution and the right persona to start off with, build, and then grow from there. And you've just got to be very particular on who you choose that are excited to be in this space because a lot of people are pragmatic, and you've got to show them 100x returns before they give you a dollar. And you don't start off with that from the beginning. So we're just trying to do it the right way.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: The other one that's probably worth having a look at – this one's a bit left field. So these guys are building – it's a company called Known, N-O-A-N. It's a lot more broad application, but I suspect you've got plans for world domination after fractionals as well. So they might be worth having a look at. They are building almost like an AI native business building platform. So you load your – they call them knowledge blocks. You put your marketing plan in there. You put your outbound plan, whatever it is, and then it flows through everything else. He started out with fractionals as well, but was unable to get enough of a beachhead to continue on it. Still has a lot of fractionals who are using it, but has gone a lot broader than that, but not a million miles away. So worth having a look at what they do, I think.
